results.title: Enforcing Certificate-Bound Authority in Agentic Control Planes - arXiv
results.content:
SAB Admission Boundary: The trusted admission gateway that intercepts proposals, verifies their safety using Semantic Quorum Assurance (SQA) or policy engines, and emits cryptographically signed certificates Ω\Omega.

Sovereign Execution Broker (SEB): The trusted execution boundary that intercepts all mutation requests, verifies certificates, checks live state, and performs execution.

Target Infrastructure: The cloud APIs (e.g., AWS, GCP) or orchestrators (e.g., Kubernetes) undergoing mutations.

Revocation Service: A service maintaining the current global policy and revocation epoch ρa​c​t​i​v​e\rho\_{active}.

Ledger: An append-only log capturing the detailed outcomes of broker decisions and executions. [...] In contrast, a Sovereign Execution Broker (SEB) evaluates a much richer context. Rather than validating static identity permissions, SEB answers:

“Is this specific, certified mutation request still valid under the current system evidence, active policy epoch, revocation epoch, live-state drift, and cryptographic scope?”

By shifting authorization from the identity to the certified action itself, SEB removes standing mutation credentials from agent runtimes when the deployment denies all non-broker mutation identities. [...] Broker Partition: The agent attempts to execute mutations during a network partition that prevents the broker from contacting the Revocation Service.

## 4 Broker Execution Model

The core of the SEB architecture is a formal verification and execution pipeline over certificates, execution requests, live target state, platform scopeability, signed DecisionRecords, and signed OutcomeRecords.

### 4.1 Broker Execution Interface

The Sovereign Execution Broker acts as a gatekeeper. It consumes an
SAB certificate Ω\Omega, a concrete execution request
r​e​qreq, the live state of the target infrastructure StS\_{t}, and a platform
descriptor that captures the target API’s native scoping mechanisms. The
interface is defined as:

results.title: Enforcing Certificate-Bound Authority in Agentic Control Planes
results.content:
Autonomous agents are increasingly connected to cloud, deployment, and data-control workflows, but production mutation authority should not reside inside non-deterministic reasoning processes. Existing access-control mechanisms authorize identities, while assurance layers certify proposed actions; neither alone provides a mandatory enforcement point for certified authority at the moment of mutation. This paper introduces the Sovereign Execution Broker (SEB), a runtime enforcement boundary for certificate-bound agentic infrastructure. SEB consumes certificates issued by the Sovereign Assurance Boundary (SAB), verifies that the requested mutation matches the certified execution contract, checks validity windows, policy epochs, revocation epochs, and live-state drift, mints scoped execution [...] epochs, and live-state drift, mints scoped execution identity, invokes infrastructure APIs, and records signed decision and outcome records. By separating proposal, admission, and execution, SEB turns certified authority into a short-lived, revocable, auditable runtime capability, provided that production mutation APIs reject non-broker identities. We present the SEB execution model, certificate and replay-verification predicates, scoped identity semantics, bypass-prevention deployment patterns, failure behavior, and a concrete prototype implementation. We evaluate the prototype on AWS and Kubernetes clusters, measuring latency overheads, revocation propagation, drift detection, and security under fault injection. [...] ### Sovereign Execution Brokers: Enforcing Certificate-Bound Authority in Agentic Control Planes

 2026-06-18 17:36:46
 Jun He, Deying Yu

results.title: Top Institutional Brokers 2025: Your Guide to Prime Brokerage Services — TradingView News
results.content:
How to Choose the Best Institutional Broker

Selecting an institutional broker involves more than comparing fees or basic trade execution. A suitable partner should align with your investment strategy, operational requirements, and long-term goals. For hedge funds, corporate treasuries, or sovereign wealth funds, the following factors are important when assessing brokers in 2025:

Service Range and Specialisation

Brokers differ in the segments they serve. Some focus on hedge funds and proprietary traders, while others work with pension funds, asset managers, or insurance companies. Understanding a broker’s core client base and the specific services they offer such as prime brokerage, execution-only access, or full-service advisory can help refine your options. [...] Sucden Financial

Sucden Financial continues to be a respected name in institutional brokerage, particularly in FX and commodity derivatives. Based in London, it caters to hedge funds, asset managers, and banks with tailored execution, clearing, and liquidity services.

In 2025, Sucden invested further in its proprietary trading infrastructure and risk analytics suite, offering clients high-speed execution and transparent post-trade reporting. With a legacy of trust and regulatory adherence, it remains a preferred choice for institutions seeking a reliable, service-focused broker.

Marex Group plc [...] Final Thoughts

In 2025, institutional brokers are increasingly expected to provide more than execution-only services. With the industry demanding high-performance infrastructure, responsive support, and multi-asset capabilities, brokers that combine technological strength with regulatory reliability stand out.Whether you require deep liquidity, access to emerging markets, or flexible API connectivity, the brokers featured in this guide offer a range of solutions suited to different strategies and operational profiles. Your choice should ultimately reflect the priorities of your firm, ensuring a broker relationship that adds long-term value to your trading and investment processes.

FAQs

What should I consider when selecting an institutional broker?
